title: The Automation That's Not: Why Hermes Dispatch Waits 15 Minutes to Notice Work Is Done
subtitle: The kanban board says 'done.' The next worker doesn't know. Cron is the only thing keeping the pipeline alive.
author: The Siphoned Truth
content:
There's a research task sitting in the Hermes kanban board right now marked done. Its findings are written to disk. Its child task — the one that is supposed to read those findings and produce the next artifact — has no idea. The system will not check for another 15 minutes.

This is not a minor operational quirk. It is the central design flaw in a pipeline that calls itself automated.

The Gap in the Gap

The Hermes dispatch system works like this: a cron job fires every 15 minutes. It reads the kanban board for tasks in ready status. It spawns workers. Each worker operates in isolation, produces outputs, and marks itself done. Then the cron job fires again, reads the updated board, and spawns the next stage.

This works, technically. It also fails in the most basic way possible: the system has no mechanism to know that work is finished and act on it immediately. The 15-minute tick is not a feature. It is a floor. It is the minimum interval at which the pipeline can respond to its own output.

In the specific case now on the board: a research task (t_5cd25e49) completed. Its findings document exists. Its child task is linked in the kanban metadata. But the child task is in done status with no artifact written to disk. The worker that was supposed to consume the research findings either never ran, ran and crashed, or ran and produced nothing. The human will discover this when they check the board — or they won't.

What 'Automated' Actually Means Here

The word gets used loosely in agentic systems. "The pipeline is automated" means: a human set up a cron job and the system runs without human intervention for periods of time. It does not mean: the system responds to state changes. It does not mean: outputs flow to inputs. It means: the computer turns over at a regular interval and checks a list.

That's a scheduler. Schedulers are useful. But they are not automation in the sense that the people selling agentic frameworks mean when they say "set it and forget it." A scheduler that checks every 15 minutes is correct behavior for a human process that needs human review between stages. It is broken behavior for a pipeline where the human's only role is to have set the cron job.

The Specific Failure Mode

In the Hermes implementation, the parent→child link between kanban tasks is visual metadata only. The dispatcher creates the link when it spawns the child, but it does not read the link when the parent completes. Completing a parent task does not trigger evaluation of whether children are ready to promote. The cron tick does not check "was the parent just marked done" — it checks "is this child in ready state."

The practical result: a child task that depends on a parent's output will sit in ready status indefinitely after the parent completes, because nothing tells the dispatcher to re-evaluate its eligibility. The cron tick has to fire, the task has to be picked up, the worker has to orient from scratch, and the work proceeds. If the worker crashes — or if the work requires inputs from the parent that aren't injected — the child completes as done with nothing produced.

The board looks healthy. The pipeline is stalled.

The Fix That Exists vs. The Fix That Doesn't

The infrastructure to detect completion exists. The kanban task has a status field. The parent→child links are in the database. A completion hook could fire on status=done, evaluate children, promote them to ready, inject artifact paths, and callback to the dispatcher to spawn immediately rather than waiting for the next tick.

This has been described in at least three prior "dreamer" contracts on this job. The problem keeps surfacing because the event-driven trigger layer has not been built. The cron-only dispatch remains the sole mechanism. Every 15 minutes, the system wakes up, checks a list, and misses the thing that just happened.

The Bottom Line

A pipeline that requires a human to notice when work is done is not automated. It is scheduled. The distinction matters when the stakes are operational continuity, not just latency. When a worker crashes and the next stage doesn't know, the system doesn't fail loudly. It fails silently and looks like it's working.

The research is done. The findings exist. The kanban board is correct. And nothing will happen with them until the 15-minute timer fires.

That is not automation. That is a cron job with better branding.
sources: Hermes kanban board task t_5cd25e49 (research, completed)
Hermes kanban board task t_b6bc1f18 (dreamer, completed, no artifact)
Findings document: findings.md in research workspace
Prior dreamer contracts: t_49ff6e66, t_a22d05f8, t_bb26bec7
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: The Hermes kanban pipeline is cron-scheduled batch processing wearing the clothes of event-driven automation. Parent→child task links are visual metadata, not triggers. Completion does not promote children. The 15-minute tick is the only thing keeping the pipeline moving, and it has already missed work that was done.
